content.text: The prototype-to-build handoff is the structured transition from lo-fi artifacts — wireframes, script prototypes, and user-tested prompts — to engineering specifications. Traceability means every engineering ticket, every eval test case, and every production decision can be traced back to a specific behavior spec element and wireframe node. Without traceability, teams routinely build features that were never specified, miss features that were specified, and cannot determine whether a production failure was caused by a specification error or an implementation error.

A prototype-to-build handoff package is the complete set of artifacts passed from the design/product phase to engineering. It is not a single document — it is a structured folder of versioned files, each with a stable ID that other artifacts can reference.
The handoff package is frozen at the point of Gate 4 approval. Any change after freeze requires a scope-change protocol review, not a casual Slack edit.

The traceability matrix is a table that links every wireframe node and every behavior spec element to the engineering ticket and eval test case that implements and validates it. It is the single most important handoff artifact — it makes scope visible and creates the audit trail that Safety and Legal reviewers need.

Prototype artifacts go through multiple rounds of user testing and revision. The handoff package must version every artifact so that engineering always knows which revision they are implementing and eval can verify against the correct version.
Major: scope change; Minor: tone/format tweak
Any change to spec text, even minor
When node attributes change (retry count, reprompt)
When expected output or turn sequence changes
On any post-freeze change (triggers scope-change review)

Each engineering ticket implementing a wireframe node must reference the handoff artifacts using a standard template. This is enforced at the sprint planning gate — tickets without required references are not eligible to enter the sprint.

# Engineering Ticket Template: GenAI Feature Implementation

## Ticket ID: ENG-<NNN>
## Title: [Handler / Component Name]

### Traceability References (all required)
- Behavior Spec: BEH-SPEC-<NN>-v<N>
- Wireframe Node: NODE-<ID>-r<N>
- Handoff Artifact: HANDOFF-<ID>
- Eval Test Cases: EVAL-<ID>-01, EVAL-<ID>-02, ...
- Safety Gate 2 Decision: [decision_id if is_safety_relevant]
- System Prompt Version: v<major>.<minor>

### Acceptance Criteria
# Derived directly from the behavior spec element and script prototype.
# Do not write ACs from scratch — copy from BEH-SPEC and adjust for code.
- [ ] Slot fills correctly for happy-path input (see EVAL-<ID>-01)
- [ ] Slot retries with correct reprompt text after first failure
- [ ] Fallback triggered after N retries (see EVAL-<ID>-02)
- [ ] Out-of-scope intent handled without revealing system prompt

### Out of Scope
# List any behavior from the wireframe that this ticket does NOT implement.
# This prevents ticket scope creep and makes review cleaner.
- Session persistence (covered by ENG-<NNN+1>)
- Analytics logging (covered by ENG-<NNN+2>)

### Definition of Done
- [ ] Unit tests pass for all ACs
- [ ] Script prototype EVAL-<ID>-01 through EVAL-<ID>-N run green
- [ ] Code reviewed against behavior spec BEH-SPEC-<NN>
- [ ] PR description links this ticket and its traceability refs

Before engineering begins, a traceability gap check identifies behavior spec elements with no engineering ticket and wireframe nodes with no eval test case. Both are blocking: an untraced spec element means the feature will be missing behavior; an untraced eval case means the feature will be tested for something no one specified.

After the handoff package is frozen, all changes — however small — must go through a documented change request. This sounds bureaucratic but prevents the most common cause of GenAI feature regressions: a prompt change made informally during development that is never reflected in the eval suite or the behavior spec.
Product owner approval, update BEH-SPEC version
New slot, changed refusal trigger, scope expansion
Full scope-change protocol: Safety + Legal re-review
Refusal policy change, guardrail threshold, HITL rule
Gate 2 re-review required regardless of scope size
Yes — frozen only after Gate 2 re-approval
The principle: if the change would have changed the user-test results or the Safety gate outcome, it needs the same review the original did. If not, it can be logged and merged without re-review.
Sprint Entry Gate: Automated Traceability Check

Many teams enforce the traceability requirement automatically at the sprint planning stage. A CI job or project management webhook checks that each ticket proposed for the sprint has all required reference fields. Tickets missing references are automatically moved back to the backlog with a comment explaining what is missing.

# sprint_gate.py — run as a pre-sprint CI check or webhook handler

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any


@dataclass
class TicketReference:
    ticket_id: str
    behavior_spec_id: str
    wireframe_node_id: str
    eval_test_case_ids: list[str]
    system_prompt_version: str
    is_safety_relevant: bool
    gate2_decision_id: str = ""

    REQUIRED_FIELDS = [
        "behavior_spec_id",
        "wireframe_node_id",
        "eval_test_case_ids",
        "system_prompt_version",
    ]

    def missing_references(self) -> list[str]:
        missing = [f for f in self.REQUIRED_FIELDS if not getattr(self, f)]
        if self.is_safety_relevant and not self.gate2_decision_id:
            missing.append("gate2_decision_id (required for safety-relevant tickets)")
        return missing

    def can_enter_sprint(self) -> tuple[bool, str]:
        missing = self.missing_references()
        if missing:
            return False, f"Missing: {', '.join(missing)}"
        return True, "All references present"


def sprint_gate_check(tickets: list[TicketReference]) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """Returns sprint gate result: which tickets pass, which are blocked."""
    passed = []
    blocked = []
    for ticket in tickets:
        ok, reason = ticket.can_enter_sprint()
        (passed if ok else blocked).append(
            {"ticket_id": ticket.ticket_id, "reason": reason}
        )
    return {
        "total": len(tickets),
        "passed": len(passed),
        "blocked": len(blocked),
        "blocked_tickets": blocked,
        "sprint_ready": len(blocked) == 0,
    }

The handoff review is a one-hour synchronous meeting attended by product, engineering lead, designer, QA lead, and the safety reviewer. Its purpose is to walk the engineering team through the traceability matrix and answer implementation questions before the sprint begins.
Engineering confirms understanding of each BEH-SPEC element
Engineering confirms handler function mapping for each node
All gaps identified and assigned to owner before sprint start
All is_safety_relevant=True rows acknowledged by engineering lead
Open questions logged; each assigned a decision owner and deadline
Any question answered in the handoff review meeting must be logged as an annotation on the relevant traceability row. Verbal answers that are not written down are not part of the specification.
Teams that skip traceability or treat it as bureaucratic overhead typically encounter a predictable set of production failures. Each failure has a direct mapping to a missing traceability link.
BEH-SPEC refusal element had no engineering ticket
Eval suite tests the wrong behavior
Eval case not linked to any spec element
Untraced-eval check flags the test before it is run
Prompt changed in production without eval update
System prompt version not referenced in ticket
Version field in ticket template; version mismatch → ticket blocked
Safety reviewer cannot find what was changed
is_safety_relevant=True + no gate2_decision_id → sprint gate rejects ticket
Model version not pinned in handoff package
ArtifactVersion records model ID; change bumps handoff version → triggers re-review

The prototype-to-build handoff converts lo-fi design artifacts into a structured, versioned package that engineering can implement and eval can verify. The traceability matrix is the core artifact — it links every behavior spec element to a wireframe node, an engineering ticket, and an eval test case. Gaps in the matrix identify scope that will be missing or untested. All artifacts are frozen on Gate 4 approval; post-freeze changes of any safety-relevant kind require re-review before the package is re-frozen.
: system prompt (locked version), behavior spec, wireframes, script prototypes, user test reports, node-to-handler map, traceability matrix
: every row links spec ID → wireframe node → engineering ticket → eval test cases; safety-relevant rows require Gate 2 decision ID
: every artifact carries a stable versioned ID; handoff package version bumped on any post-freeze change
: all tickets must reference behavior spec, wireframe node, and eval cases — tickets without these refs cannot enter the sprint
: before engineering begins, detect spec elements without tickets, spec elements without eval, and eval cases not traced to any spec element
: cosmetic → log only; minor behavioral → product owner + version bump; major or safety-relevant → full re-review + re-freeze
